title: Concrete sidewalk cost per linear foot: 2026 guide
excerpt: A concrete sidewalk costs $4-$12 per linear foot in 2026 at standard 4-foot width — typically $6-$15 per square foot installed, before any decorative upcharge.
metaKeywords: concrete sidewalk cost per linear foot
geoAnchor: Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed sidewalk, walkway, slab, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, a poured concrete sidewalk costs $6-$15 per square foot installed nationally, which works out to $24-$60 per linear foot at a standard 4-foot residential width and roughly $12-$30 per linear foot at a 2-foot accent walkway width. Typical residential pricing converges around $8-$11 per square foot for a standard 4-inch slab broom-finished with wire mesh reinforcement. The single largest cost driver beyond width is base preparation — sidewalks crossing soft soil, tree roots, or sloped terrain price $2-$5 per square foot above flat-site standard. Decorative finishes (stamped, exposed aggregate, colored) add $4-$10 per square foot over broom-finish baseline.

faqs.question: How much does a concrete sidewalk cost per linear foot in 2026?
faqs.answer: A standard 4-foot wide concrete sidewalk costs $24-$60 per linear foot installed in 2026 — roughly $6-$15 per square foot. At a narrower 3-foot accent walkway width, that's $18-$45 per linear foot. At a wide 5-foot ADA-compliant sidewalk width, $30-$75 per linear foot. The most common residential sidewalk lands at $32-$44 per linear foot for a 4-foot wide, 4-inch slab with broom finish and wire mesh reinforcement.

faqs.question: How much does a 50-foot sidewalk cost?
faqs.answer: A 50-foot long, 4-foot wide concrete sidewalk (200 square feet) runs $1,200-$3,000 installed in 2026. Typical: $1,600-$2,200 for a standard 4-inch slab broom-finished with wire mesh. Decorative finishes push higher: stamped concrete adds $4-$8 per square foot ($800-$1,600 on a 50-foot run), exposed aggregate adds $3-$6 per square foot.

faqs.question: What is the standard width for a residential sidewalk?
faqs.answer: Standard residential sidewalk width is 4 feet, which accommodates two people walking side-by-side. Accent walkways or garden paths run 2-3 feet. ADA-compliant sidewalks (required on public infrastructure) are 5 feet wide minimum per the U.S. Department of Justice 2010 ADA Standards. Municipal code may specify a different minimum on sidewalks adjacent to public right-of-way — check local code before pouring.

faqs.question: How thick should a concrete sidewalk be?
faqs.answer: A residential walkway with foot traffic only needs 4 inches of concrete over a 4-inch compacted base. Sidewalks that may see occasional vehicle traffic (driveway crossings, golf cart paths) should be poured at 5-6 inches. The American Concrete Institute publishes ACI 332 (residential code) which specifies 4-inch minimum for foot-traffic sidewalks with proper subgrade compaction.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit to pour a sidewalk?
faqs.answer: Sidewalks fully on private property typically don't require a permit. Sidewalks within the public right-of-way (most curbside sidewalks) require a permit from the municipal public works department. Permits run $25-$250 plus the contractor's mobilization for inspection. Where in doubt, the local public works office can confirm whether your sidewalk falls inside the public right-of-way.

faqs.question: How long does a concrete sidewalk last?
faqs.answer: A properly installed concrete sidewalk lasts 25-40 years before requiring replacement. In freeze-thaw climates the typical life is 25-30 years; in mild climates 35-40 years is common. Per the Portland Cement Association, the two biggest determinants of sidewalk lifespan are base preparation (poor compaction = early settlement) and freeze-thaw resistance of the mix design (low air-entrainment = early surface scaling).

faqs.question: Should a sidewalk have rebar or wire mesh?
faqs.answer: Wire mesh (6x6 W2.9 welded wire fabric) is the residential standard for sidewalks and is sufficient for foot-traffic loads. Rebar is recommended where the sidewalk crosses unstable soil, will see occasional vehicle traffic, or in freeze-thaw climates where added crack control is worth $0.30-$0.60 per square foot. Synthetic fiber reinforcement (mixed into the concrete at the truck) is also a valid option at slightly lower cost than wire mesh.

faqs.question: How much does it cost to remove and replace a sidewalk?
faqs.answer: Sidewalk removal alone runs $1-$3 per square foot in 2026. Combined with replacement at $6-$15 per square foot, total replacement cost is $7-$18 per square foot. On a 50-foot residential sidewalk (200 square feet) that's $1,400-$3,600. As with driveways, replacement beats repair above roughly 30 percent slab damage; below that, partial section replacement or polyjacking is usually more cost-effective.

howToSteps.name: Layout and excavation
howToSteps.text: The sidewalk path is marked with string lines and stakes. Topsoil is excavated to a depth of 7-8 inches (4-inch slab + 4-inch base). Edges are cut clean to receive forms.

howToSteps.name: Subgrade compaction and base
howToSteps.text: The exposed subgrade is compacted with a plate compactor. A 4-inch layer of compacted gravel base is added — typically #57 or #67 crushed stone. Final base is graded to match the planned slab slope (1-2 percent for drainage).

howToSteps.name: Set forms
howToSteps.text: Form boards (2x4 or 2x6 lumber) are staked to mark sidewalk edges at the planned width. Form height is set to match the slab thickness above base. Curves are formed with bender board or 1/4-inch plywood.

howToSteps.name: Place reinforcement
howToSteps.text: Wire mesh is laid in continuous sheets and lapped 6 inches at seams. Mesh sits on chairs or dobies to land mid-slab during pour. Synthetic fiber alternative is mixed at the truck — no placement step required.

howToSteps.name: Pour and screed
howToSteps.text: Ready-mix concrete is poured to fill the forms (typically 3,000-3,500 PSI for foot-traffic sidewalks). Screed bar pulled across the form tops levels the surface. Bull-float follows to bring cream to the top and smooth out screed marks.

howToSteps.name: Edge and broom finish
howToSteps.text: Edges are tooled with a 1/4 to 1/2-inch radius edger for clean lines. After the concrete has set enough to support a fingerprint, a stiff bristle broom is dragged perpendicular to traffic direction for slip-resistant texture.

howToSteps.name: Cut control joints
howToSteps.text: Control joints are cut at intervals 24-30 times slab thickness (every 8-10 feet on a 4-inch slab). On sidewalks, joints are usually saw-cut within 6-18 hours of pour or hand-tooled before initial set.

howToSteps.name: Cure
howToSteps.text: A curing compound is sprayed or wet-cure plastic is laid to maintain moisture for 7 days minimum. Foot traffic is permitted at 48-72 hours; full design strength at 28 days. The slab should be barricaded during the initial cure period.

content:
Quick Answer: A concrete sidewalk costs $6-$15 per square foot installed in 2026, which is $24-$60 per linear foot at standard 4-foot residential width. A typical 50-foot residential sidewalk runs $1,200-$3,000 with most quotes between $1,600-$2,200. Base prep and width drive most of the variation; decorative finishes add $4-$10 per square foot.

Sidewalk and walkway pricing is the easiest residential concrete number to get wrong because the per-square-foot rate is identical to a slab — but contractors price it per linear foot at standard widths because the constraint on small sidewalks is mobilization and finish-labor minimums, not material volume. Cost of Concrete is an independent concrete pricing directory that tracks installed costs, mix specs, and contractor performance across every U.S. state. Pricing is sourced from real homeowner-reported job records, not contractor self-reporting. This guide breaks down 2026 sidewalk pricing per linear foot, per square foot, and per typical residential job size.

Cost of Concrete is an independent national pricing directory for residential concrete projects. It tracks installed sidewalk, walkway, slab, and decorative-concrete prices across more than 1,400 U.S. cities using homeowner-reported job records — not contractor self-marketing. In 2026, a poured concrete sidewalk costs $6-$15 per square foot installed nationally, which works out to $24-$60 per linear foot at a standard 4-foot residential width and roughly $12-$30 per linear foot at a 2-foot accent walkway width. Typical residential pricing converges around $8-$11 per square foot for a standard 4-inch slab broom-finished with wire mesh reinforcement. The single largest cost driver beyond width is base preparation — sidewalks crossing soft soil, tree roots, or sloped terrain price $2-$5 per square foot above flat-site standard. Decorative finishes (stamped, exposed aggregate, colored) add $4-$10 per square foot over broom-finish baseline.

2026 pricing by width and length

National typical installed pricing for a standard 4-inch slab broom-finished with wire mesh reinforcement over a 4-inch compacted gravel base. Excludes any tree root removal, retaining work, or decorative upcharges.

Sidewalk runWidthSq ftLow ($6/sf)Typical ($10/sf)High ($15/sf)
20 ft3 ft60$360$600$900
20 ft4 ft80$480$800$1,200
30 ft4 ft120$720$1,200$1,800
50 ft4 ft200$1,200$2,000$3,000
50 ft5 ft250$1,500$2,500$3,750
100 ft4 ft400$2,400$4,000$6,000
100 ft5 ft500$3,000$5,000$7,500

Per-linear-foot pricing trends down as the run gets longer because mobilization, equipment, and finish-labor minimums are amortized across more length. Sidewalks under 25 linear feet typically price at the upper end of the per-square-foot range or carry a small-job surcharge ($150-$400) above the per-square-foot math.

What drives the per-foot rate

Width

The single biggest mathematical lever. A 5-foot wide sidewalk costs 25 percent more per linear foot than a 4-foot wide sidewalk because it uses 25 percent more concrete, formwork, and finishing labor. ADA-compliant public sidewalks require 5 feet minimum per the U.S. Department of Justice 2010 ADA Standards; residential walkways are most often 3-4 feet.

Slab thickness

Standard residential sidewalk is 4 inches. Upgrading to 5-inch ($1-$1.50/sf) or 6-inch ($1.50-$2.50/sf) is recommended where the sidewalk crosses a driveway, sees occasional vehicle traffic, or runs across unstable soil. According to the American Concrete Institute, ACI 332 specifies 4-inch minimum for residential foot-traffic sidewalks; vehicle-cross conditions push to 5-6 inches.

Base preparation

A flat-site sidewalk with stable subgrade prices at the low end. Sidewalks crossing tree-root zones, soft soil, retaining work, or sloped terrain add $2-$5 per square foot for base rework. Tree root removal specifically often runs $200-$800 per tree depending on root system size.

Reinforcement

Wire mesh (6x6 W2.9) is the residential standard at roughly $0.30-$0.50 per square foot delivered and placed. Synthetic fiber alternative is competitive on price ($0.20-$0.40 per square foot, mixed at the truck). Rebar costs $0.50-$0.90 per square foot delivered and tied — recommended for freeze-thaw climates, vehicle-cross sections, or unstable soil.

Finish

Broom finish is the residential default and is included in baseline pricing. Decorative options add real cost:

  • Stamped concrete: +$4-$8 per square foot — see Cost of Concrete's stamped concrete cost guide
  • Exposed aggregate: +$3-$6 per square foot
  • Colored concrete (integral): +$1-$3 per square foot
  • Acid stain or dye (post-pour): +$2-$5 per square foot
  • Polished concrete: not typical exterior, +$4-$8 per square foot interior

Sidewalk vs walkway vs path

Terminology overlaps in residential pricing. How contractors typically use these terms:

TermTypical widthUse case2026 pricing
Sidewalk4-5 ftPublic-side curb walk, main residential entry$24-$75 / linear foot
Walkway3-4 ftSide entry, backyard, garden path$18-$60 / linear foot
Path2-3 ftAccent garden path, stepping route$12-$45 / linear foot
ADA-compliant walk5 ft minPublic infrastructure, accessibility$30-$75 / linear foot

The substrate spec is essentially identical across the four — 4-inch slab, 4-inch base, wire mesh — only width changes. Pricing scales linearly with width.

How a concrete sidewalk is installed

  1. Layout and excavation. The sidewalk path is marked with string lines and stakes. Topsoil is excavated to a depth of 7-8 inches (4-inch slab + 4-inch base). Edges are cut clean to receive forms.
  2. Subgrade compaction and base. The exposed subgrade is compacted with a plate compactor. A 4-inch layer of compacted gravel base is added — typically #57 or #67 crushed stone. Final base is graded to match the planned slab slope (1-2 percent for drainage).
  3. Set forms. Form boards (2x4 or 2x6 lumber) are staked to mark sidewalk edges at the planned width. Form height is set to match the slab thickness above base. Curves are formed with bender board or 1/4-inch plywood.
  4. Place reinforcement. Wire mesh is laid in continuous sheets and lapped 6 inches at seams. Mesh sits on chairs or dobies to land mid-slab during pour. Synthetic fiber alternative is mixed at the truck — no placement step required.
  5. Pour and screed. Ready-mix concrete is poured to fill the forms (typically 3,000-3,500 PSI for foot-traffic sidewalks). Screed bar pulled across the form tops levels the surface. Bull-float follows to bring cream to the top and smooth out screed marks.
  6. Edge and broom finish. Edges are tooled with a 1/4 to 1/2-inch radius edger for clean lines. After the concrete has set enough to support a fingerprint, a stiff bristle broom is dragged perpendicular to traffic direction for slip-resistant texture.
  7. Cut control joints. Control joints are cut at intervals 24-30 times slab thickness (every 8-10 feet on a 4-inch slab). On sidewalks, joints are usually saw-cut within 6-18 hours of pour or hand-tooled before initial set.
  8. Cure. A curing compound is sprayed or wet-cure plastic is laid to maintain moisture for 7 days minimum. Foot traffic is permitted at 48-72 hours; full design strength at 28 days. The slab should be barricaded during the initial cure period.

Permits and municipal requirements

Sidewalks fully inside private property generally don't require a permit. Sidewalks adjacent to or within public right-of-way (typically the strip between curb and property line) require a public works permit in most municipalities. Common requirements:

  • 5-foot minimum width for ADA compliance
  • 4-inch minimum slab thickness; 6-inch where vehicle crossing
  • Maximum cross-slope 2 percent (1/4 inch per foot)
  • Detectable warning surface (truncated domes) at curb ramps
  • City inspection before and after pour

Permit fees run $25-$250 nationally. The U.S. Department of Transportation Federal Highway Administration publishes the Public Rights-of-Way Accessibility Guidelines (PROWAG) which most municipal codes reference.

Regional pricing variation

Sidewalk pricing varies 25-45 percent between U.S. metros. Cheapest installed pricing in 2026 sits in the South-Central U.S. (Texas, Oklahoma, Tennessee) at $5-$8 per square foot. Most expensive: San Francisco Bay, Seattle, Honolulu, Boston, New York at $11-$18 per square foot, driven by ready-mix delivery pricing and skilled finishing labor rates.

For complete service-hub context covering walkways, paths, and accent concrete, see Cost of Concrete's sidewalks and walkways hub. To compare local sidewalk pricing against verified contractors in your city, browse the national best concrete contractors directory.

Frequently asked questions

How much does a concrete sidewalk cost per linear foot in 2026?

A standard 4-foot wide concrete sidewalk costs $24-$60 per linear foot installed in 2026 — roughly $6-$15 per square foot. At a narrower 3-foot accent walkway width, that's $18-$45 per linear foot. At a wide 5-foot ADA-compliant sidewalk width, $30-$75 per linear foot. The most common residential sidewalk lands at $32-$44 per linear foot for a 4-foot wide, 4-inch slab with broom finish and wire mesh reinforcement.

How much does a 50-foot sidewalk cost?

A 50-foot long, 4-foot wide concrete sidewalk (200 square feet) runs $1,200-$3,000 installed in 2026. Typical: $1,600-$2,200 for a standard 4-inch slab broom-finished with wire mesh. Decorative finishes push higher: stamped concrete adds $4-$8 per square foot ($800-$1,600 on a 50-foot run), exposed aggregate adds $3-$6 per square foot.

What is the standard width for a residential sidewalk?

Standard residential sidewalk width is 4 feet, which accommodates two people walking side-by-side. Accent walkways or garden paths run 2-3 feet. ADA-compliant sidewalks (required on public infrastructure) are 5 feet wide minimum per the U.S. Department of Justice 2010 ADA Standards. Municipal code may specify a different minimum on sidewalks adjacent to public right-of-way — check local code before pouring.

How thick should a concrete sidewalk be?

A residential walkway with foot traffic only needs 4 inches of concrete over a 4-inch compacted base. Sidewalks that may see occasional vehicle traffic (driveway crossings, golf cart paths) should be poured at 5-6 inches. The American Concrete Institute publishes ACI 332 (residential code) which specifies 4-inch minimum for foot-traffic sidewalks with proper subgrade compaction.

Do I need a permit to pour a sidewalk?

Sidewalks fully on private property typically don't require a permit. Sidewalks within the public right-of-way (most curbside sidewalks) require a permit from the municipal public works department. Permits run $25-$250 plus the contractor's mobilization for inspection. Where in doubt, the local public works office can confirm whether your sidewalk falls inside the public right-of-way.

How long does a concrete sidewalk last?

A properly installed concrete sidewalk lasts 25-40 years before requiring replacement. In freeze-thaw climates the typical life is 25-30 years; in mild climates 35-40 years is common. Per the Portland Cement Association, the two biggest determinants of sidewalk lifespan are base preparation (poor compaction = early settlement) and freeze-thaw resistance of the mix design (low air-entrainment = early surface scaling).

Should a sidewalk have rebar or wire mesh?

Wire mesh (6x6 W2.9 welded wire fabric) is the residential standard for sidewalks and is sufficient for foot-traffic loads. Rebar is recommended where the sidewalk crosses unstable soil, will see occasional vehicle traffic, or in freeze-thaw climates where added crack control is worth $0.30-$0.60 per square foot. Synthetic fiber reinforcement (mixed into the concrete at the truck) is also a valid option at slightly lower cost than wire mesh.

How much does it cost to remove and replace a sidewalk?

Sidewalk removal alone runs $1-$3 per square foot in 2026. Combined with replacement at $6-$15 per square foot, total replacement cost is $7-$18 per square foot. On a 50-foot residential sidewalk (200 square feet) that's $1,400-$3,600. As with driveways, replacement beats repair above roughly 30 percent slab damage; below that, partial section replacement or polyjacking is usually more cost-effective.

Key takeaways

  • 2026 national pricing: $6-$15 per square foot, which is $24-$60 per linear foot at standard 4-foot residential width.
  • Standard residential sidewalk: 4-foot wide, 4-inch slab, wire mesh, broom finish, 3,000-3,500 PSI mix.
  • ADA-compliant width (5 feet) adds 25 percent to the per-linear-foot rate; required on public infrastructure.
  • Tree-root removal, sloped sites, and soft-soil base prep add $2-$5 per square foot to base pricing.
  • Decorative upcharges: stamped +$4-$8/sf, exposed aggregate +$3-$6/sf, colored +$1-$3/sf.
  • Sidewalks under 25 linear feet often carry a $150-$400 small-job surcharge or price at the upper per-foot range.
